assignments.title: CUDA Programming Model
assignments.summary: Covers Chapter 5: Kernels, thread hierarchy, thread block clusters, memory hierarchy, heterogeneous programming, asynchronous SIMT, and compute capabilities.
assignments.reason: Detailed explanation of CUDA's core parallel execution and memory abstractions.

assignments.title: CUDA Programming Interface & NVCC Compilation
assignments.summary: Covers Chapter 6: Programming interface overview, C++ extensions, runtime library, and nvcc compilation workflow.
assignments.reason: Practical interface and compilation details for developers.

assignments.title: Just-in-Time Compilation
assignments.summary: Explains how PTX code is compiled to binary at runtime by the device driver, the benefits of JIT compilation, the compute cache mechanism, and its invalidation policy.
assignments.reason: Covers section 6.1.1.2 detailing the JIT compilation process and cache management.

assignments.title: Binary Compatibility
assignments.summary: Details architecture-specific binary code generation, cubin objects, and compatibility guarantees across minor and major compute capability revisions.
assignments.reason: Covers section 6.1.2 explaining binary compatibility rules and limitations.

assignments.title: Application Compatibility
assignments.summary: Describes how applications load compatible binary or PTX code, compiler options (-arch, -code, -gencode), runtime code selection, and macros for differentiating code paths.
assignments.reason: Covers section 6.1.4 detailing application compatibility mechanisms and compiler directives.

assignments.title: C++ Compatibility
assignments.summary: States that CUDA source files follow C++ syntax rules, with full C++ support for host code and a subset for device code.
assignments.reason: Covers section 6.1.5 on C++ language support boundaries.

assignments.title: 64-Bit Compatibility
assignments.summary: Explains that the 64-bit nvcc compiles device code in 64-bit mode, which requires host code to also be compiled in 64-bit mode.
assignments.reason: Covers section 6.1.6 on 64-bit compilation requirements.

assignments.title: CUDA Runtime
assignments.summary: Introduces the cudart library, its linking methods, symbol prefixing, and provides an overview of runtime features like device memory, shared memory, async execution, and graphics interoperability.
assignments.reason: Covers section 6.2 providing the high-level introduction to the CUDA Runtime API and its capabilities.

assignments.title: CUDA Runtime Initialization
assignments.summary: Details runtime initialization behavior in CUDA 12.0+, context creation, cudaSetDevice() changes, and warnings about global state during program initiation/termination.
assignments.reason: Covers section 6.2.1 focusing specifically on initialization and context management.

assignments.title: CUDA Device Memory
assignments.summary: Explains device memory allocation (linear memory vs CUDA arrays), address space sizes, cudaMalloc/cudaFree/cudaMemcpy usage, pitch/3D allocations, and global/constant symbol access.
assignments.reason: Covers section 6.2.2 detailing device memory management and API functions.

assignments.title: L2 Cache Access Management
assignments.summary: Describes L2 cache set-aside for persisting accesses, access policy windows, hit ratios, access properties (streaming, persisting, normal), and API functions for querying and controlling L2 cache behavior.
assignments.reason: Covers section 6.2.3 and its subsections detailing L2 cache persistence and policy management.

assignments.title: Shared Memory
assignments.summary: Explains shared memory allocation using __shared__, its performance benefits over global memory, and provides detailed matrix multiplication examples demonstrating shared memory usage for tiling and synchronization.
assignments.reason: Covers section 6.2.4 detailing shared memory concepts and optimization examples.

assignments.title: Distributed Shared Memory
assignments.summary: Shared memory partitioned across thread blocks within a cluster (compute capability 9.0+), enabling cross-block read/write/atomic operations via cooperative_groups API.
assignments.reason: Covers section 6.2.5 and all associated code examples and launch configurations for distributed shared memory.

assignments.title: Page-Locked Host Memory
assignments.summary: Host memory allocated with cudaHostAlloc or cudaHostRegister that prevents paging, enabling concurrent transfers, device mapping, and higher bandwidth. Includes portable, write-combining, and mapped memory variants.
assignments.reason: Covers sections 6.2.6 through 6.2.6.3, detailing APIs, benefits, and subtypes (portable, write-combining, mapped) of pinned host memory.

assignments.title: Memory Synchronization Domains
assignments.summary: Hopper architecture (CUDA 12.0+) feature that isolates memory traffic into logical domains to reduce fence/flush interference. Uses launch attributes to map logical domains to physical domains and manage cross-domain system-scope fencing.
assignments.reason: Covers sections 6.2.7 through 6.2.7.3, explaining fence interference, domain isolation mechanics, and API usage (cudaLaunchAttributeMemSyncDomain, cudaLaunchAttributeMemSyncDomainMap).

assignments.title: Asynchronous Concurrent Execution
assignments.summary: CUDA capability to execute host/device computations, memory transfers, and kernel launches concurrently. Covers host-device concurrency, concurrent kernel execution limits, and overlapping data transfers with kernel execution.
assignments.reason: Covers sections 6.2.8 through 6.2.8.4, detailing asynchronous operations, concurrency limits, device properties (concurrentKernels, asyncEngineCount), and transfer overlap requirements.

assignments.title: CUDA Streams
assignments.summary: Ordered sequences of commands (kernels, copies) that enable concurrent execution across multiple streams. Covers stream lifecycle, dependency management, and resource cleanup behavior.
assignments.reason: Covers sections 6.2.8.5 through 6.2.8.5.1, focusing on stream creation, execution ordering, concurrency guarantees, and destruction semantics.

assignments.title: Default Stream
assignments.summary: Kernel launches and memory copies without a stream parameter use the default stream, executing in order. Compilation flags like --default-stream per-thread or legacy alter whether the default stream is per-thread or a shared NULL stream.
assignments.reason: Covers section 6.2.8.5.2 explaining default stream behavior and compilation flags.

assignments.title: Explicit Synchronization
assignments.summary: Methods to explicitly synchronize streams include cudaDeviceSynchronize(), cudaStreamSynchronize(), cudaStreamWaitEvent(), and cudaStreamQuery().
assignments.reason: Covers section 6.2.8.5.3 detailing explicit synchronization APIs.

assignments.title: Implicit Synchronization
assignments.summary: Operations from different streams cannot run concurrently if a CUDA operation on the NULL stream is submitted between them, unless non-blocking streams are used. Guidelines recommend issuing independent operations first and delaying synchronization.
assignments.reason: Covers section 6.2.8.5.4 explaining implicit synchronization rules and best practices.

assignments.title: Stream Overlap Behavior
assignments.summary: Execution overlap between streams depends on command order and device support for overlapping data transfers and kernel execution. Proper ordering can hide launch latency and enable concurrent execution.
assignments.reason: Covers section 6.2.8.5.5 detailing how stream overlap works and code examples.

assignments.title: Host Functions (Callbacks)
assignments.summary: cudaLaunchHostFunc() inserts a CPU function call into a stream that executes after preceding commands complete. Commands issued after a callback wait for it, and callbacks must not make CUDA API calls to avoid deadlock.
assignments.reason: Covers section 6.2.8.5.6 explaining host function callbacks and their constraints.

assignments.title: Stream Priorities
assignments.summary: Stream priorities can be set at creation using cudaStreamCreateWithPriority(). The GPU scheduler uses these as hints to prioritize higher-priority tasks, but they do not preempt running tasks or guarantee strict ordering.
assignments.reason: Covers section 6.2.8.5.7 explaining stream priority ranges, scheduler behavior, and code examples.

assignments.title: Programmatic Dependent Launch
assignments.summary: A mechanism (compute capability 9.0+) allowing a secondary kernel to launch before its primary kernel finishes in the same stream. Uses cudaTriggerProgrammaticLaunchCompletion() and cudaGridDependencySynchronize() for safe concurrent execution. Supported in CUDA Graphs via edge data.
assignments.reason: Covers section 6.2.8.6 and 6.2.8.6.1-6.2.8.6.3 detailing the API, background, and CUDA Graph integration.

assignments.title: CUDA Graphs
assignments.summary: A model for work submission separating graph definition, instantiation, and execution to reduce CPU overhead and enable optimizations. Covers node types, edge data, creation via APIs or stream capture, cross-stream dependencies, user objects, and graph update mechanisms.
assignments.reason: Covers section 6.2.8.7 and all subsections (6.2.8.7.1 to 6.2.8.7.5.2) detailing the complete CUDA Graphs feature set.

assignments.title: Individual Node Update
assignments.summary: Covers updating parameters of instantiated graph nodes directly using specific setter APIs to avoid instantiation overhead. Applicable when the number of nodes to update is small relative to the total graph size.
assignments.reason: Section 6.2.8.7.5.3 details the concept and API list for individual node parameter updates.

assignments.title: Individual Node Enable
assignments.summary: Covers enabling or disabling specific kernel, memset, and memcpy nodes within an instantiated graph using `cudaGraphNodeSetEnabled()`. Disabled nodes act as empty nodes until re-enabled, and parameter updates while disabled apply upon re-enablement.
assignments.reason: Section 6.2.8.7.5.4 explains the enable/disable mechanism and associated query API.

assignments.title: Graph API Usage Guidelines
assignments.summary: Covers thread-safety rules for `cudaGraph_t` (not thread-safe, user responsibility), concurrency limits for `cudaGraphExec_t` (cannot run concurrently with itself, ordered launches), and the role of streams for ordering without constraining internal graph parallelism.
assignments.reason: Section 6.2.8.7.6 outlines fundamental usage constraints and stream behavior for CUDA graphs.

assignments.title: Device Graph Launch Overview
assignments.summary: Covers the concept of device graphs, which allow dynamic control flow and decision-making directly on the GPU without host round-trips. Requires unified addressing. Distinguishes between device graphs (launchable from host/device) and host graphs (host-only). Notes concurrency restrictions: device graphs cannot be launched twice from the device simultaneously, and simultaneous host/device launches cause undefined behavior.
assignments.reason: Section 6.2.8.7.7 introduces the device graph launch workflow and constraints.

assignments.title: Device Graph Creation
assignments.summary: Covers the explicit instantiation requirement for device graphs using the `cudaGraphInstantiateFlagDeviceLaunch` flag. Structure is fixed at instantiation, and instantiation must occur on the host.
assignments.reason: Section 6.2.8.7.7.1 details the creation process and flags.

assignments.title: Device Graph Requirements
assignments.summary: Covers constraints for device graphs: single-device nodes, allowed node types (kernel, memcpy, memset, child graph), restrictions on CUDA Dynamic Parallelism, MPS cooperative launch limits, memory access rules for memcpy/memset (device/pinned host memory only, no CUDA arrays), and operand accessibility at instantiation.
assignments.reason: Section 6.2.8.7.7.2 lists strict hardware and API requirements for device graphs.

assignments.title: Device Graph Upload
assignments.summary: Covers methods to upload device graphs to the GPU to populate device resources: explicit upload via `cudaGraphUpload()`, upload during instantiation via `cudaGraphInstantiateWithParams()`, or implicit upload via an initial host launch.
assignments.reason: Section 6.2.8.7.7.3 explains the upload mechanisms with code examples.

assignments.title: Device Graph Update
assignments.summary: Covers that device graphs can only be updated from the host and require re-upload to the device for changes to take effect. Launching from the device during an update causes undefined behavior.
assignments.reason: Section 6.2.8.7.7.4 details update constraints.

assignments.title: Device Launch Execution
assignments.summary: Covers launching device graphs from host or device using `cudaGraphLaunch()`. Notes that device-side launch is per-thread, requiring users to select a single thread for a given graph to avoid conflicts.
assignments.reason: Section 6.2.8.7.7.5 explains the launch execution model.

assignments.title: Device Launch Modes
assignments.summary: Covers the three distinct named streams for device graph launch modes: Fire and Forget, Tail Launch, and Fire and Forget As Sibling. Device graphs cannot be launched into regular CUDA streams.
assignments.reason: Section 6.2.8.7.7.6 defines the launch mode stream constants.

assignments.title: Fire and Forget Launch
assignments.summary: Covers the fire-and-forget mode where the launched graph runs independently as a child of the parent graph's execution environment. Limits up to 120 fire-and-forget graphs per parent execution, resetting between launches.
assignments.reason: Section 6.2.8.7.7.7 details the fire-and-forget workflow, environment hierarchy, and limits.

assignments.title: Graph Execution Environments
assignments.summary: Covers the hierarchical execution environment model for device-side synchronization. Environments encapsulate graph work and generated fire-and-forget work. Host launches create a parent stream environment. Completion is marked when the graph and all child work finish.
assignments.reason: Section 6.2.8.7.7.8 explains the synchronization and environment encapsulation model.

assignments.title: Tail Launch
assignments.summary: Covers the tail launch mode for serial work dependencies on the device. Executes when the parent graph's environment completes. Supports multiple enqueued graphs, strict ordering, and up to 255 pending tail launches per graph.
assignments.reason: Section 6.2.8.7.7.9 details the tail launch mechanism, ordering, and limits.

assignments.title: Tail Self-Launch
assignments.summary: Covers the ability of a device graph to enqueue itself for a tail launch using `cudaGetCurrentGraphExec()`. Allows device-side looping/recursion. Only one self-launch can be enqueued at a time.
assignments.reason: Section 6.2.8.7.7.10 explains self-launching graphs and the query API.

assignments.title: Sibling Launch
assignments.summary: Covers sibling launch mode, where the graph is launched as a child of the parent environment rather than the launching graph's environment. Does not gate tail launches enqueued by the launching graph.
assignments.reason: Section 6.2.8.7.7.11 details the sibling launch variation and its synchronization implications.

assignments.title: Conditional Graph Nodes Overview
assignments.summary: Covers conditional nodes (IF, WHILE, SWITCH) that enable dynamic control flow and looping within graphs. Evaluation happens on-device after dependencies are met. Uses condition handles created before the node, with values set via `cudaGraphSetConditional()` or defaults.
assignments.reason: Section 6.2.8.7.8 introduces conditional nodes, their types, and handle mechanism.

assignments.title: Conditional Handles
assignments.summary: Covers `cudaGraphConditionalHandle` creation and lifecycle. Handles are tied to a single node, cannot be destroyed, and can be initialized with a default value using `cudaGraphCondAssignDefault`. Defaults update during whole-graph updates.
assignments.reason: Section 6.2.8.7.8.1 details handle creation and initialization flags.

assignments.title: Conditional Node Body Graph Requirements
assignments.summary: Covers constraints on the body graphs of conditional nodes: single-device nodes, allowed node types (kernel, empty, memcpy, memset, child graph, conditional), restrictions on Dynamic Parallelism/Device Graph Launch, MPS limits, and memory access rules matching device graph requirements.
assignments.reason: Section 6.2.8.7.8.2 lists requirements specific to conditional node body graphs.

assignments.title: Conditional IF Nodes
assignments.summary: Covers IF conditional nodes that execute a body graph once if the condition is non-zero. Supports an optional second body graph (else branch) executed if zero (added in CUDA 12.8). Includes creation and population examples.
assignments.reason: Section 6.2.8.7.8.3 details IF node behavior and provides code examples.

assignments.title: Conditional WHILE Nodes
assignments.summary: Covers WHILE conditional nodes that execute their body graph repeatedly as long as the condition remains non-zero. Condition is evaluated at execution and after each body completion. Includes creation and loop example.
assignments.reason: Section 6.2.8.7.8.4 details WHILE node behavior and provides code examples.

assignments.title: Conditional SWITCH Nodes
assignments.summary: SWITCH nodes, introduced in CUDA 12.8, allow execution of one of n different graphs within a conditional node based on an evaluated condition value. If the condition value is greater than or equal to n, no graph is executed. The section provides code examples for creating graphs with SWITCH conditional nodes using the CUDA Graph API.
assignments.reason: Covers the definition, behavior, and code implementation of Conditional SWITCH Nodes in CUDA graphs.

assignments.title: Conditional SWITCH Nodes
assignments.reason: Empty line between sections.

assignments.title: CUDA Events
assignments.summary: Events provide a mechanism to monitor device progress and perform accurate timing by asynchronously recording events at any program point. Events complete when all preceding tasks or commands in a stream finish. The section covers event creation, destruction, and measuring elapsed time between events.
assignments.reason: Covers the creation, destruction, and elapsed time measurement of CUDA events.

assignments.title: Synchronous Calls
assignments.summary: Synchronous functions block the host thread until the device completes the requested task. Host thread behavior (yield, block, or spin) can be configured using cudaSetDeviceFlags() before any CUDA calls.
assignments.reason: Explains the behavior and configuration of synchronous CUDA API calls.

assignments.title: Synchronous Calls
assignments.reason: Section header without content.

assignments.title: Device Enumeration
assignments.summary: Host systems can contain multiple devices. This section demonstrates how to enumerate CUDA-enabled devices, query their properties, and determine the total device count using cudaGetDeviceCount() and cudaGetDeviceProperties().
assignments.reason: Covers querying and enumerating available CUDA devices.

assignments.title: Device Selection
assignments.summary: Host threads can set the active device using cudaSetDevice(). All memory allocations and kernel launches occur on the currently set device. If unset, device 0 is used by default.
assignments.reason: Explains how to select and switch between multiple CUDA devices.

assignments.title: Device Selection
assignments.reason: Continuation note from previous page.

assignments.title: Stream and Event Behavior
assignments.summary: Details the behavior of streams and events across different devices. Kernel launches fail if issued to a stream not associated with the current device, while memory copies may succeed. Events and streams on different devices have specific synchronization and error-checking rules. Default streams on different devices may execute concurrently.
assignments.reason: Covers cross-device behavior of streams, events, and synchronization.

assignments.title: Peer-to-Peer Memory Access
assignments.summary: Devices can directly address each other's memory based on PCIe/NVLINK topology. Enabled via cudaDeviceEnablePeerAccess() and verified with cudaDeviceCanAccessPeer(). Requires 64-bit applications and unified address space. Linux bare-metal systems require IOMMU to be disabled for PCIe P2P, while VM pass-through with VFIO is supported.
assignments.reason: Covers enabling and configuring direct device-to-device memory access.

assignments.title: Peer-to-Peer Memory Copy
assignments.summary: Memory can be copied directly between devices using cudaMemcpyPeer() or similar functions. Copies in the implicit NULL stream synchronize with all previous commands. Asynchronous copies can overlap with other operations. P2P access via cudaDeviceEnablePeerAccess() avoids staging through host memory, improving performance.
assignments.reason: Covers direct memory copying between devices and its synchronization/performance characteristics.

assignments.title: Unified Virtual Address Space
assignments.summary: In 64-bit processes, a single address space covers the host and all devices (compute capability 2.0+). Pointers can be queried for location using cudaPointerGetAttributes(), and cudaMemcpyDefault can be used for transfers. cudaHostAlloc() creates portable memory accessible directly by kernels without explicit device pointer conversion.
assignments.reason: Covers the unified address space model, pointer attributes, and portable host memory.

assignments.title: Interprocess Communication
assignments.summary: CUDA IPC API allows sharing device memory pointers and events across processes using cudaIpcGetMemHandle() and cudaIpcOpenMemHandle(). Supports 64-bit Linux and compute capability 2.0+. Memory sharing may expose sub-allocations, so 2MiB aligned allocations are recommended. L4T/Tegra devices have limited IPC support (events only for compute 7.x+).
assignments.reason: Covers sharing CUDA resources across different processes.

assignments.title: Error Checking
assignments.summary: Runtime functions return error codes, but asynchronous functions report errors via subsequent calls. cudaDeviceSynchronize() is required to catch async errors. cudaPeekAtLastError() and cudaGetLastError() manage thread-local error variables. Kernel launches require explicit error checking immediately after launch, and cudaErrorNotReady is not considered an error.
assignments.reason: Covers CUDA error handling mechanisms, synchronization for async errors, and error variable management.

assignments.title: Call Stack
assignments.summary: Call stack size can be queried and set on compute capability 2.x+ devices using cudaDeviceGetLimit() and cudaDeviceSetLimit(). Stack overflow causes kernel failure or warnings, especially for recursive functions. Manual stack size configuration is required when the compiler cannot determine it statically.
assignments.reason: Covers querying, setting, and managing the GPU call stack size.

assignments.title: Texture and Surface Memory
assignments.summary: CUDA provides access to texture and surface memory for performance benefits over global memory. Texture objects specify texture dimensions, texel types, read modes, coordinate normalization, addressing modes, and filtering modes (point or linear). Textures support 1D, 2D, and 3D addressing with various coordinate transformations and interpolation methods.
assignments.reason: Covers the configuration and properties of texture and surface memory access.

The API supports advanced features like anisotropic filtering, mipmapping, and sRGB. The section provides a complete code example demonstrating texture allocation, configuration, kernel execution, and cleanup.

assignments.title: Surface Memory

assignments.title: Surface Object API
assignments.summary: Explains surface object creation via cudaCreateSurfaceObject(), byte-addressing differences compared to texture memory, and provides a complete code sample for a simple copy kernel using surf2Dread/surf2Dwrite, including host setup, array allocation, surface creation, kernel launch, and cleanup.

assignments.title: Cubemap Surfaces
assignments.summary: Describes cubemap surface access using surfCubemapread() and surfCubemapwrite() as a two-dimensional layered surface, referencing face ordering from Table 6.

assignments.title: Cubemap Layered Surfaces
assignments.summary: Describes cubemap layered surface access using surfCubemapLayeredread() and surfCubemapLayeredwrite() as a two-dimensional layered surface, explaining index calculation for faces across multiple cubemaps.

assignments.title: CUDA Arrays
assignments.summary: Defines CUDA arrays as opaque, optimized memory layouts for texture fetching (1D, 2D, 3D) with various component types. Notes they are only accessible via texture fetching or surface read/write operations.
assignments.reason: Covers the entire subsection 6.2.14.3

assignments.title: Read/Write Coherency
assignments.summary: Explains that texture and surface memory is cached and not kept coherent with global/surface writes within the same kernel call. Reads return undefined data if written to in the same kernel call, requiring updates from previous kernel calls or memory copies.
assignments.reason: Covers the entire subsection 6.2.14.4

assignments.title: Graphics Interoperability
assignments.summary: Overview of mapping OpenGL and Direct3D resources into CUDA address space. Covers registration (cudaGraphicsRegisterResource), mapping/unmapping, usage hints, accessing mapped pointers/arrays, and restrictions on concurrent access.
assignments.reason: Covers the entire subsection 6.2.15

assignments.title: OpenGL Interoperability
assignments.summary: Details mapping OpenGL buffers, textures, and renderbuffers to CUDA. Covers registration functions (cudaGraphicsGLRegisterBuffer/Image), usage as device pointers or CUDA arrays, surface load/store flags, context requirements, bindless texture limitations, and provides a complete code sample for dynamic vertex buffer modification.
assignments.reason: Covers the entire subsection 6.2.15.1

assignments.title: Direct3D Interoperability
assignments.summary: Covers Direct3D 9Ex, 10, and 11 interoperability. Specifies device creation requirements (HAL, hardware vertex processing, D3D_DRIVER_TYPE_HARDWARE). Lists registered resources (buffers, textures, surfaces) and registration functions (cudaGraphicsD3D9/10/11RegisterResource).
assignments.reason: Covers the entire subsection 6.2.15.2

assignments.title: Direct3D 9 Version
assignments.summary: Provides a complete code sample for Direct3D 9 interoperability. Covers D3D device creation, CUDA device selection, vertex buffer registration, mapping/unmapping, kernel execution for vertex modification, and resource cleanup.
assignments.reason: Covers the entire subsection 6.2.15.2.1

assignments.title: Direct3D 10 Interoperability
assignments.summary: Demonstrates how to create a Direct3D 10 device, register a vertex buffer with CUDA, map it for GPU writing, launch a kernel to generate vertex data, and unmap it for rendering.
assignments.reason: Covers section 6.2.15.2.2, detailing the complete workflow for CUDA-Direct3D 10 interoperability including device enumeration, resource registration, mapping, kernel execution, and cleanup.

assignments.title: Direct3D 11 Interoperability
assignments.summary: Shows the equivalent workflow for Direct3D 11, including device creation, buffer registration via cudaGraphicsD3D11RegisterResource, mapping, kernel execution, and resource unregistration.
assignments.reason: Covers section 6.2.15.2.3, providing the D3D11-specific API calls and code structure for interoperability, which differs slightly from D3D10 in device creation and registration functions.

assignments.title: SLI Interoperability
assignments.summary: Explains special considerations for CUDA interoperability in SLI configurations, including memory allocation impacts across GPUs, the recommendation to create one CUDA context per GPU, and using cudaD3D[9|10|11]GetDevices() or cudaGLGetDevices() to map resources to the correct device per frame.
assignments.reason: Covers section 6.2.15.3, which details multi-GPU SLI constraints, context management, and device selection for frame-accurate resource mapping.

assignments.title: External Resource Interoperability
assignments.summary: Introduces the framework for importing external memory and synchronization objects (semaphores) from other APIs using OS-native handles or NVIDIA SWI. Covers cudaImportExternalMemory(), cudaExternalMemoryGetMappedBuffer(), cudaImportExternalSemaphore(), and their lifecycle management.
assignments.reason: Covers section 6.2.16, providing the foundational API and concepts for cross-API resource sharing via external memory and semaphore objects.

assignments.title: Formatted Output
assignments.summary: Formatted output is supported on compute capability 2.x and higher via the in-kernel printf() function. It behaves similarly to the standard C library printf() but outputs to a host-side stream. Each thread executes printf independently. The function returns the number of arguments parsed. Limitations include host-dependent formatting, a 32-argument limit, potential output corruption on win64 with %ld, and a fixed-size circular buffer that flushes on kernel launch, synchronization, memory copies, module loading, context destruction, or stream callbacks. Thread execution order may be affected.

assignments.title: Dynamic Global Memory Allocation and Operations
assignments.summary: Dynamic global memory allocation is supported on compute capability 2.x and higher. Functions include __device__ malloc(), __nv_aligned_device_malloc(), free(), memcpy(), and memset(). Memory is allocated from a fixed-size device heap (default 8 MB, configurable via cudaDeviceSetLimit). Allocated memory persists for the context lifetime and can be shared across threads and kernel launches. It cannot be freed via host-side runtime APIs, nor can host-allocated memory be freed via device free(). Examples cover per-thread, per-block, and cross-kernel-persistence allocation patterns.
assignments.reason: Covers the CUDA device-side dynamic memory allocation functions, heap management, interoperability rules, and usage examples.

assignments.summary: Introduces CUDA Unified Memory, its system requirements, programming model, allocation APIs (cudaMallocManaged, __managed__), and examples demonstrating memory allocation and usage across host and device.
assignments.reason: Covers Chapter 24 detailing Unified Memory Programming, including introduction, system requirements, programming model, and allocation APIs.

assignments.title: Difference between Unified Memory and Mapped Memory
assignments.summary: Explains the core difference: Mapped Memory lacks guaranteed support for all memory operations (e.g., atomics) across systems, while Unified Memory guarantees portability for these operations.
assignments.reason: Distinct conceptual comparison section.

assignments.title: CUDA Pointer Attributes
assignments.summary: Details the use of cudaPointerGetAttributes() to identify pointer types (cudaMemoryTypeManaged, cudaMemoryTypeHost, cudaMemoryTypeUnregistered) and clarifies that attributes indicate allocation/registration method, not physical location. Includes a runtime detection code example.
assignments.reason: Covers the API and logic for querying pointer attributes.

assignments.title: Runtime Detection of Unified Memory Support Level
assignments.summary: Provides a code example demonstrating how to query device attributes (cudaDevAttrPageableMemoryAccess, cudaDevAttrConcurrentManagedAccess) at runtime to determine the level of Unified Memory support available on the system.
assignments.reason: Self-contained section on runtime capability detection.

assignments.title: GPU Memory Oversubscription
assignments.summary: Explains how Unified Memory allows applications to allocate and share arrays larger than individual processor memory capacities, enabling out-of-core processing without significant model complexity.
assignments.reason: Brief conceptual definition of oversubscription capability.

assignments.title: Unified Memory Performance Hints
assignments.summary: Introduces performance hints (cudaMemPrefetchAsync, cudaMemAdvise, etc.) that allow applications to provide CUDA with additional information to optimize data placement and migration without altering application semantics.
assignments.reason: Introductory section for the performance hints subsections.

assignments.title: Data Prefetching in CUDA
assignments.summary: Describes cudaMemPrefetchAsync, an asynchronous stream-ordered API to migrate data closer to a specified processor (GPU or CPU). Includes examples for System Allocator and Managed memory prefetching workflows.
assignments.reason: Covers the specific API and usage patterns for data prefetching.

assignments.title: Memory Discarding in CUDA
assignments.summary: Details cudaMemDiscardBatchAsync and cudaMemDiscardAndPrefetchBatchAsync APIs. Explains how marking memory ranges as discarded prevents redundant migrations during faults/evictions, improving performance. Includes important considerations regarding indeterminate reads and concurrent access.
assignments.reason: Covers the discard optimization APIs and their constraints.

assignments.title: Data Usage Hints in CUDA
assignments.summary: Explains cudaMemAdvise and its values: cudaMemAdviseSetReadMostly, cudaMemAdviseSetPreferredLocation, and cudaMemAdviseSetAccessedBy. Provides code examples and explains how these hints guide the Unified Memory driver for optimal data placement and migration.
assignments.reason: Covers the primary API for advising the driver on access patterns.

assignments.title: Querying Data Usage Attributes
assignments.summary: Describes cudaMemRangeGetAttribute and cudaMemRangeGetAttributes APIs used to query attributes set via cudaMemAdvise or cudaMemPrefetchAsync. Lists queryable attributes like ReadMostly, PreferredLocation, AccessedBy, and LastPrefetchLocation.
assignments.reason: Covers the API for checking previously set memory advice.

assignments.title: System-Allocated Memory Examples
assignments.summary: Demonstrates advanced use-cases for System-Allocated Memory on devices with full Unified Memory support. Includes examples for malloc, cudaMallocManaged, stack variables, file-scope/static variables, global-scope variables, extern variables, and file-backed memory (mmap).
assignments.reason: Covers a comprehensive set of examples showing how different memory scopes and allocations interact with the GPU.

assignments.title: Inter-Process Communication with Unified Memory
assignments.summary: Explains that CUDA IPC does not support Managed Memory, but System-Allocated Memory on full-support systems is IPC-capable. Lists Linux mechanisms (mmap MAP_SHARED, POSIX IPC, memfd_create) for sharing memory across processes.
assignments.reason: Distinct section on IPC limitations and workarounds for Unified Memory.

assignments.title: Unified Memory Performance Tuning
assignments.summary: Provides general advice for tuning Unified Memory performance: understanding paging, keeping data local, tuning for transfer granularity, and carefully using performance hints to avoid overhead.
assignments.reason: Introductory overview of the performance tuning chapter.

assignments.title: Memory Paging and Page Sizes
assignments.summary: Defines virtual vs. physical pages, page sizes (4KiB, 2MiB, etc.), page tables, and TLBs. Explains how virtual addressing and paging mechanics impact Unified Memory performance tuning.
assignments.reason: Foundational concepts required for understanding subsequent tuning sections.

assignments.title: Choosing the Right Page Size
assignments.summary: Analyzes the trade-offs between small and large virtual page sizes regarding memory fragmentation, TLB misses, and migration latency. Highlights that GPU TLB misses are particularly costly.
assignments.reason: Specific subsection on page size selection strategy.

assignments.title: Hardware vs. Software Coherency in Page Tables
assignments.summary: Contrasts hardware coherent systems (combined CPU/GPU page tables) with software coherent systems (separate page tables requiring page faults for coherency). Explains performance benefits of hardware coherency regarding TLB misses, cache-line granularity contention, and atomic updates.
assignments.reason: Covers the architectural distinction and its performance implications.

assignments.title: Direct Unified Memory Access from Host
assignments.summary: Describes cudaDevAttrDirectManagedMemAccessFromHost, which enables hardware-coherent direct reads/stores/atomics from host to GPU-resident memory without page faults. Notes the requirement for cudaMemAdviseSetAccessedBy with cudaMemLocationTypeHost.
assignments.reason: Covers the specific attribute and mechanism for host direct access.

assignments.title: Managed Memory Direct Access & System Allocator vs Managed Memory Behavior
assignments.summary: Covers the difference between system-allocated memory and managed memory, and how the directManagedMemAccessFromHost hardware feature affects CPU/GPU memory migration and faulting behavior.
assignments.reason: Covers the System Allocator and Managed Memory sections, including code examples and the explanation of directManagedMemAccessFromHost=1 vs 0 behavior.

assignments.title: Host Native Atomics
assignments.summary: Describes hardware-accelerated atomic accesses to CPU-resident memory on NVLink-connected coherent systems, eliminating the need for page fault emulation.
assignments.reason: Dedicated section on host native atomics and the corresponding device attribute.

assignments.title: Unified Memory Atomic Accesses & Synchronization Primitives
assignments.summary: Covers CUDA Unified Memory support for atomic operations, heterogeneous synchronization primitives, and page fault behavior on systems with/without CPU and GPU page tables.
assignments.reason: Covers the Atomic accesses & synchronization primitives section, including code examples and page fault implications.

assignments.title: Memcpy()/Memset() Behavior With Unified Memory
assignments.summary: Explains how cudaMemcpy*() and cudaMemset*() accept unified memory pointers, the role of cudaMemcpyKind as a performance hint, and best practices for performance optimization.
assignments.reason: Covers the dedicated section on memcpy/memset behavior with unified memory and performance advice.

assignments.title: Unified Memory on Devices with Compute Capability 6.x+
assignments.summary: Describes full support for CUDA Managed Memory on devices with compute capability 6.x or higher but without pageable memory access, noting the exclusion of system allocators.
assignments.reason: Covers the specific subsection detailing unified memory support on CC 6.x+ devices.

assignments.title: Unified Memory on Windows or Compute Capability 5.x
assignments.summary: Covers Managed Memory v1.0 support on Windows and devices with compute capability lower than 6.0, highlighting limited data migration, coherency, and oversubscription support.
assignments.reason: Covers the dedicated subsection for legacy Windows and CC 5.x platforms.

assignments.title: Data Migration and Coherency on Pre-6.x Architectures
assignments.summary: Explains the lack of fine-grained data movement on pre-6.x GPUs, the introduction of the GPU page faulting mechanism in CC 6.x, and system-wide virtual address space benefits.
assignments.reason: Covers the specific subsection on data migration and coherency mechanisms for older architectures.

assignments.title: GPU Memory Oversubscription on Pre-6.x
assignments.summary: States the limitation that devices with compute capability lower than 6.0 cannot allocate more managed memory than the physical size of GPU memory.
assignments.reason: Covers the dedicated subsection on oversubscription limits for older hardware.

assignments.title: Multi-GPU Managed Memory on Pre-6.x
assignments.summary: Details managed memory visibility across GPUs via peer-to-peer, Linux driver migration behavior, Windows zero-copy fallback, and the CUDA_MANAGED_FORCE_DEVICE_ALLOC environment variable.
assignments.reason: Covers the multi-GPU subsection, including Linux/Windows differences and environment variables.

assignments.title: Coherency and Concurrency Constraints on Pre-6.x
assignments.summary: Explains that simultaneous CPU/GPU access is not possible on pre-6.x GPUs due to coherency guarantees, the role of the concurrentManagedAccess property, and the requirement for explicit synchronization.
assignments.reason: Covers the Coherency and Concurrency section, including code examples demonstrating segmentation faults and synchronization requirements.

assignments.title: Explicit Synchronization and Logical GPU Activity
assignments.summary: Details how Unified Memory uses logical activity to determine GPU idle state, valid synchronization functions, stream dependencies, and constraints on concurrent CPU/GPU access.
assignments.reason: Covers the subsection on explicit synchronization, logical activity, and stream/event synchronization rules.

assignments.title: Managing Data Visibility with cudaStreamAttachMemAsync
assignments.summary: Explains how cudaStreamAttachMemAsync() associates managed allocations with specific streams to enable finer-grained CPU/GPU concurrency and data transfer optimizations.
assignments.reason: Covers the subsection introducing stream attachment for visibility control and concurrency.

assignments.title: Stream Association Examples and Visibility Rules
assignments.summary: Provides code examples demonstrating stream attachment, host-attach flags, and explains the conservative default global visibility behavior when allocations are not stream-attached.
assignments.reason: Covers the examples and detailed explanation of stream attachment visibility rules.

assignments.title: Stream Attach With Multithreaded Host Programs
assignments.summary: Describes using cudaStreamAttachMemAsync() for independent task parallelism in multi-threaded programs, the role of the cudaMemAttachHost flag, and modular program constraints.
assignments.reason: Covers the multithreading and modular program subsections, including code examples and flag usage.

assignments.title: Memcpy()/Memset() Behavior With Stream-associated Unified Memory
assignments.summary: Details the rules for cudaMemcpy*() and cudaMemset*() operations on stream-associated unified memory, including coherency checks, stream activity constraints, and error conditions.
assignments.reason: Covers the dedicated subsection on copy/memset behavior specifically for stream-associated memory.

assignments.title: Lazy Loading in CUDA
assignments.summary: Covers the entire Chapter 25 on Lazy Loading, including its purpose, version support (Driver/Toolkit/Compiler), triggering mechanisms, querying the loading mode, and potential issues like concurrent execution deadlocks, VRAM allocation conflicts, and autotuning warmup requirements.
assignments.reason: Covers the complete Chapter 25 section on Lazy Loading, which is a distinct, comprehensive feature/topic.

assignments.title: Extended GPU Memory
assignments.summary: CUDA feature enabling GPUs to access system memory via NVLink-C2C/NVSwitch, supporting single-node and multi-node topologies with VMM and Memory Pool APIs.
assignments.reason: Covers the entire Chapter 26 content on Extended GPU Memory, including topology, NUMA identifiers, allocators, and API usage examples.

assignments.title: Extended GPU Memory
assignments.summary: Legal notices, OpenCL trademark, NVIDIA trademarks, and copyright information.
assignments.reason: Pure boilerplate, legal disclaimers, and copyright text with no wiki content value.
